--

Build a reactive file upload flow with validation, HTTP communication, and modern Angular patterns.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

File uploads are one of those features every Angular application eventually needs. Whether you’re uploading profile pictures, invoices, PDFs, or media assets, the implementation usually ends up being more complex than expected.
Most tutorials still demonstrate file uploads with Reactive Forms, but Angular 22 introduces Signal Forms, a modern API that makes form state easier to manage while keeping your code reactive and type-safe.
In this article, we’ll build a simple file handling flow using Angular 22 Signal Forms that covers:

• Selecting files

• Client-side validation

• Displaying validation errors

• Uploading files using HttpClient
Let’s dive in.

Why Signal Forms?
Reactive Forms have served Angular applications for years, but they often require additional boilerplate such as FormGroup, FormControl, and subscriptions to react to changes.
Signal Forms simplify this by making your model the single source of truth.
Instead of synchronizing a form with your state, the form is created directly from a signal.
const uploadModel = signal({
files: [] as File[]
});const uploadForm = form(uploadModel);Whenever the model changes, the UI stays synchronized automatically.
No subscriptions.
No patchValue().
No valueChanges.

Creating the Upload Model
We’ll begin with a minimal model that stores the selected files.
import { signal } from '@angular/core';export const uploadModel = signal({
files: [] as File[],
});
Now create a Signal Form.
import { form } from '@angular/forms/signals';export const uploadForm = form(uploadModel);
The form() function creates a FieldTree, giving you strongly typed access to every property in your model.

Selecting Files
Unlike text inputs, browsers don’t allow binding <input type="file"> directly to a form field.
Instead, we handle the file selection event and update our model.
onFileSelected(event: Event): void {
const input = event.target as HTMLInputElement;if (!input.files) {
return;
}
this.uploadModel.update(model => ({
...model,
files: Array.from(input.files),
}));
}
Template:
<input
type="file"
multiple
accept=".png,.jpg,.jpeg,.pdf"
(change)="onFileSelected($event)"
>Since the selected files are stored inside the signal model, every dependent UI updates automatically.

Validating Selected Files
Before sending files to your API, it’s important to validate them on the client.
A good upload experience usually validates:

• Required selection

• Maximum file size

• Maximum number of files

• Allowed MIME types
For example:
const MAX_SIZE = 5 * 1024 * 1024;const ALLOWED_TYPES = [
'image/png',
'image/jpeg',
'application/pdf'
];
You can then filter invalid files before updating the model.
const validFiles = Array.from(input.files).filter(file =>
ALLOWED_TYPES.includes(file.type) &&
file.size <= MAX_SIZE
);this.uploadModel.update(model => ({
...model,
files: validFiles,
}));
Client-side validation improves the user experience, but it should never replace server-side validation.
Always validate uploaded files on the backend as well.

Displaying Validation State
One of my favorite features of Signal Forms is that every field exposes its own reactive state.
You can access values, errors, and validation status directly.
uploadForm.files().value()uploadForm.files().errors()
uploadForm.files().valid()
There’s no need to subscribe to changes or manually trigger UI updates.
Your template simply reacts to the current state.

Uploading Files with HttpClient
Once validation succeeds, create a FormData object.
const formData = new FormData();this.uploadModel().files.forEach(file => {
formData.append('files', file);
});
Sending the request is straightforward.
this.http.post('/api/files/upload', formData).subscribe({
next: () => {
console.log('Files uploaded successfully.');
},
error: (error) => {
console.error(error);
}
});Using FormData allows the browser to send files as multipart/form-data, which is the standard format expected by most APIs.

Why I Like This Approach
The complete flow is surprisingly simple.
Select Files
│
▼
Validate
│
▼
Update Signal Model
│
▼
Create FormData
│
▼
Send HTTP RequestEach step has a single responsibility.
Your Signal Form manages state.
HttpClient handles communication.
The browser handles file selection.
Everything stays clean and predictable.

Best Practices
Here are a few recommendations I follow when implementing file uploads:

• Keep the Signal Form model as the single source of truth.

• Validate file size and MIME type before uploading.

• Always validate uploaded files on the server.

• Use FormData instead of manually serializing files.

• Keep HTTP communication inside a dedicated service for larger applications.

• Display meaningful validation messages instead of generic upload failures.

Final Thoughts
Signal Forms make file handling feel much more natural.
Instead of managing multiple pieces of form state with FormGroup, FormControl, and subscriptions, you work directly with a reactive model that represents your application's data.
For file uploads, this leads to simpler code, better readability, and a cleaner separation between validation, state management, and HTTP communication.
If you’re starting a new Angular 22 project, Signal Forms are definitely worth exploring — especially for real-world features like file handling.
Happy coding! 🚀
